project_title: Pedagogical Friction in the Age of Generative AI
qualifying_paper_title: Beyond Secondary Orality: Tertiary Algorithmicity and the Case for Pedagogical Friction

overview.abstract_short: The completed qualifying paper develops the conceptual argument that generative AI requires extending Walter Ong's media ecology beyond secondary orality. It proposes algorithmic secondary orality and tertiary algorithmicity, then offers pedagogical friction as an educational response framework focused on preserving the noetic, rhetorical, existential, and infrastructural conditions necessary for durable learning. The dissertation now examines how that framework holds up in educator and institutional practice.
overview.central_problem: The central educational problem posed by generative AI is the bypassing of interpretive labor, intellectual accountability, and developmental conditions for learning rather than academic integrity alone.
key_contributions: Identifies three broken assumptions in Ong's framework under contemporary algorithmic media: human origination of symbolic content, transparent distribution logics, and media as external environment.

Proposes two extensions: algorithmic secondary orality (human creation plus algorithmic circulation) and tertiary algorithmicity (algorithmic creation plus curation; human authorship optional at scale).
Develops three defining characteristics of tertiary algorithmicity: noetic displacement, rhetorical saturation, and existential abstraction.
Synthesizes media ecology with learning sciences (productive failure, desirable difficulties, cognitive load) to propose pedagogical friction.
Introduces a productive vs. exclusionary friction distinction to avoid reproducing inequity under the guise of rigor.

stages.label: Primary Orality
stages.summary: Memory-based, communal, situational thought; knowledge preserved through repetition and formulaic expression.
stages.source_anchor: In primary oral cultures, the noetic world is memory-based, communal, and situational. Knowledge must be preserved through repetition.

stages.label: Literacy
stages.summary: Writing externalizes memory; enables analytical detachment, abstraction, critique, and private interiority.
stages.source_anchor: By separating the knower from the known, writing makes possible increasingly articulate introspectivity.

stages.label: Secondary Orality
stages.summary: Broadcast media retrieves participatory oral qualities within literate infrastructure.
stages.source_anchor: Secondary orality emerges with electronic broadcast media, yet depends on literate infrastructure like scripts and schedules.

stages.label: Algorithmic Secondary Orality
stages.summary: Humans still create symbolic content while algorithms increasingly determine its circulation via opaque personalization.
stages.source_anchor: Humans continued to create symbolic content, but algorithms increasingly determined what content reached which consciousness.

stages.label: Tertiary Algorithmicity
stages.summary: Algorithmic systems both curate and generate symbolic content, rendering human authorship optional at scale.
stages.source_anchor: Algorithmic systems both create and curate symbolic content, making human authorship optional at scale.

concepts.title: Algorithmic Secondary Orality
concepts.definition: A transitional phase where human-created content persists but algorithmic feeds and recommendation systems determine what reaches which people.
key_points: Fragmentation of shared symbolic environment as two users see different feeds.
Editorial function shifts from visible human judgment to opaque engagement-optimized ranking.

concepts.title: Tertiary Algorithmicity
concepts.definition: A condition in which algorithmic systems generate as well as curate symbolic content, so authorship is not necessarily human.
key_points: Qualitative rupture from distribution to generation.
Oral or literate modality matters less than human vs. algorithmic origination.

concepts.title: Noetic Displacement
concepts.definition: Cognitive labor is externalized from storage and retrieval to generation; interpretive operations are offloaded to external systems.
key_points: Students can submit competent analysis without engaging in interpretive labor.
Raises the practical question: what has the student learned?

concepts.title: Rhetorical Saturation
concepts.definition: Communicative environments flood with synthetic discourse; origin uncertainty rises and dialogic quality degrades.
key_points: Simulated dialogue offers responsiveness without genuine contestation.

concepts.title: Existential Abstraction
concepts.definition: Symbolic production detaches from lived experience and intellectual stakes; claims can circulate without an accountable claimant.
key_points: Connection between claim and claimant dissolves.
Students can produce without investing and avoid intellectual risk.

concepts.title: Pedagogical Friction
concepts.definition: Intentional preservation of the cognitive, dialogic, authorial, and institutional conditions necessary for durable learning when automation trends toward frictionless production.
key_points: Not a ban or embrace stance; it is design for necessary difficulty.
Protect productive struggle while reducing exclusionary obstacles.
Treat noetic, rhetorical, and existential friction as learner-facing dimensions, with infrastructural friction as the system layer that enables or constrains them.

concepts.title: Productive vs. Exclusionary Friction
concepts.definition: A distinction to prevent preserving struggle from becoming inequitable: some difficulty builds schema, while some blocks participation.
key_points: Translation can remove exclusionary friction while also flattening voice.
Determination requires situated professional judgment, not a universal rule.

framework_dimensions.label: Noetic Friction (The Head)
framework_dimensions.definition: A learner-facing dimension: internal cognitive resistance required for comprehension, synthesis, argumentation, and revision; aligns with germane load, generation effect, and productive failure.

framework_dimensions.label: Rhetorical Friction (The Room)
framework_dimensions.definition: A learner-facing dimension: dialogic struggle of defending ideas with unpredictable real audiences; includes peer review, discussion, and oral defense.

framework_dimensions.label: Existential Friction (The World)
framework_dimensions.definition: A learner-facing dimension: embodied accountability and vulnerability of authorship, including being personally answerable for claims.

framework_dimensions.label: Infrastructural Friction (The System)
framework_dimensions.definition: The institutional condition of possibility for the learner-facing dimensions: policy, assessment, leadership, governance, and professional learning conditions that enable or constrain noetic, rhetorical, and existential friction.
examples: AI policy that names productive struggle

qualifying_paper: What does Ong's media ecology make newly visible about algorithmic curation and generative AI as transformations of the communication environment?
What are the educational consequences when algorithmic systems do more than mediate and actually generate the symbolic environments in which students learn?
What theoretical framework can guide educators in preserving the cognitive, rhetorical, and existential conditions necessary for durable learning in an age of generative AI?

dissertation.RQ1: How do classroom-facing educators, building-level administrators, district or system-level leaders, and adult university students make sense of the ways generative AI changes the effort, dialogue, authorship, judgment, and participation involved in teaching and learning?
dissertation.RQ2: How do educators and school-system leaders make sense of and respond to the policy, professional-learning, assessment, access, and governance conditions surrounding generative AI?
dissertation.RQ3: What language, assessment expectations, instructional practices, professional supports, and leadership approaches do participants identify as important for preserving forms of friction that support learning while reducing unnecessary barriers?

evidence_status.adult_university_students: Adult university students are a participant group whose retrospective accounts provide a learner perspective on teaching and learning under generative AI.
evidence_status.ai_text_comparison: AI-generated texts are analyzed as a distinct nonparticipant comparison source within the study and are never treated as evidence about human experience.

design.approach: Qualitative-dominant convergent mixed methods design grounded in constructivist qualitative inquiry.
design.constructivist_stance: Meaning is treated as situated and co-constructed. The Pedagogical Friction Framework serves as a sensitizing and revisable lens rather than a container for the inquiry.

participants: Adult university students as a participant group

sources: NCES School Pulse Panel as structural context
RAND American Educator Panel and RAND/Gallup data as structural context

integration: Explicit preservation of convergence, divergence, expansion, and silence

ai_comparison_indicators.label: Temporal authenticity if learner-perspective prompts are retained
